title: OccAny: Generalized Unconstrained Urban 3D Occupancy
summary: OccAny is introduced as the first generalized 3D occupancy prediction model designed to operate on unconstrained, out-of-domain, and uncalibrated urban scenes, addressing the limitations of prior methods requiring precise sensor priors. The framework is versatile, handling sequential, monocular, or surround-view image inputs to predict metric occupancy coupled with segmentation features. Key methodological contributions include 'Segmentation Forcing' for improved occupancy quality and mask prediction, and a 'Novel View Rendering' pipeline for geometry completion via test-time augmentation. Experiments show OccAny surpasses general visual geometry baselines while maintaining strong performance against in-domain self-supervised approaches on urban occupancy benchmarks.

title: Active learning-enabled multi-objective design of thermally conductive and mechanically compliant polymers
summary: This paper introduces an Active Learning (AL) framework powered by Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization (MOBO) to overcome the challenge of simultaneously achieving high thermal conductivity (TC) and mechanical compliance in polymers. The methodology uses a Molecular Dynamics (MD) pipeline to generate initial data, feeding Deep Kernel Learning (DKL) models that predict both properties and uncertainties. The framework iteratively employs the parallel noisy expected hypervolume improvement (qNEHVI) acquisition function to intelligently sample a large polymer database, minimizing required simulations. The approach successfully identified six Pareto-optimal polymer candidates, providing a scalable, data-efficient workflow for multifunctional material design.

title: Foveated Diffusion: Efficient Spatially Adaptive Image and Video Generation
summary: Foveated Diffusion addresses the quadratic complexity bottleneck in generating high-resolution images and videos by exploiting the non-uniform resolution sensitivity of human vision. The approach uses an estimated or known gaze location to construct a mixed-resolution token map, allocating higher token density (detail) to the foveal region and lower density to the periphery. A mechanism is proposed to allow existing diffusion models to be efficiently post-trained on these mixed-resolution tokens, maintaining perceptual quality while substantially cutting down on token count and generation time. Extensive evaluation and user studies confirm that the resulting outputs are perceptually equivalent to full-resolution generations using significantly fewer resources.

title: Dynamic Light Spanners in Doubling Metrics
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of dynamically maintaining a $(1+\varepsilon)$-spanner for a set of points undergoing insertions and deletions within a metric space of constant doubling dimension. The authors introduce an algorithm that performs each update (insertion or deletion) in $\operatorname{poly}(\log \Phi)$ time, where $\Phi$ is the aspect ratio of the point set. Crucially, the resulting spanner is light-weight, maintaining a total weight within a constant factor of the weight of the current Minimum Spanning Tree (MST). This result resolves a significant open problem by providing an efficient dynamic spanner maintenance solution, even for low-dimensional Euclidean spaces where prior efficient methods were lacking.

title: One View Is Enough! Monocular Training for In-the-Wild Novel View Generation
summary: The paper introduces OVIE, a novel method for monocular novel view synthesis that eliminates the need for multi-view supervision by training entirely on unpaired internet images. The core innovation involves using a pre-trained monocular depth estimator during training to generate pseudo-target views through 3D lifting and projection, guided by a masked loss formulation to handle disocclusions. Critically, the geometry module is discarded at inference, making the system geometry-free, fast, and robust. OVIE demonstrates state-of-the-art zero-shot performance and is significantly faster than prior methods, validating the hypothesis that one view is sufficient for high-quality synthesis.

title: Stablecoins as Dry Powder: A Copula-Based Risk Analysis of Cryptocurrency Markets
summary: This paper investigates the under-explored role of stablecoins in transmitting systemic risk within cryptocurrency and Decentralised Finance (DeFi) markets by employing copula-based methods. The authors quantify the causality of volatility and activity flowing from stablecoins to the wider crypto ecosystem across multiple time horizons. Key findings indicate that stablecoin factors, specifically volume and upside volatility, significantly improve forecasting accuracy for cryptocurrency returns by reducing MSE. Economically, incorporating these factors enhances risk management, as demonstrated by reduced risk in a volatility targeting framework, solidifying the view of stablecoins acting as market "dry powder."

title: Statistical Efficiency of Single- and Multi-step Models for Forecasting and Control
summary: This work analyzes the trade-off between single-step and multi-step forecasting models in the context of learning-based control, specifically addressing the issue of compounding error. The authors compare single-step models, direct multi-step models, and single-step models trained with multi-step losses for linear dynamical systems. They establish that when the system dynamics are perfectly captured (well-specified), single-step models yield the lowest asymptotic prediction error. Conversely, direct multi-step predictors are advantageous when the model class suffers from misspecification due to partial observability, as they effectively reduce bias. These findings concerning prediction accuracy are shown to hold true when the models are deployed in closed-loop control.

title: PNap: Lifecycle-aware Edge Multi-state sleep for Energy Efficient MEC
summary: This paper introduces PowerNap (PNap), a lifecycle-aware orchestration framework designed to reduce the significant energy consumption associated with keeping multiple Multi-access Edge Computing (MEC) servers active during low-load phases. PNap jointly manages the server's multi-state sleep modes and the execution lifecycle of hosted services, which have non-instantaneous wake-up requirements. By leveraging traffic forecasting, the framework optimizes the system to minimize the count of active servers while preventing service continuity disruptions. Evaluation against baseline and state-of-the-art methods shows that PNap reduces energy consumption by up to 14.9% while achieving comparable service availability.

title: Granger Causality in Expectiles: an M-vine copula test
summary: This paper introduces a novel, model-free measure of Granger causality specifically defined in terms of expectiles, extending traditional mean-based causality to any point in the conditional distribution, which is useful for risk-sensitive forecasting. The authors construct a statistical test based on this measure by employing M-vine copula models to capture the multivariate, non-linear, and non-Gaussian dependence structure among $d+1$ time series. The key advantage is the capacity for joint testing, allowing the detection of complex causal structures overlooked by pairwise approaches, with the strong consistency of the test statistic formally established. Applications to international stock market indices validate the practical utility of the proposed framework for distributional causality analysis.

title: WaveSFNet: A Wavelet-Based Codec and Spatial--Frequency Dual-Domain Gating Network for Spatiotemporal Prediction
summary: WaveSFNet is proposed as an efficient, recurrent-free framework for spatiotemporal prediction designed to overcome the loss of high-frequency details common in downsampling methods. The core innovation is a unified architecture combining a wavelet-based codec, which maintains high-frequency subband cues, and a spatial-frequency dual-domain gated translator. This translator enhances dynamic information using adjacent-frame differences and fuses large-kernel spatial modeling with frequency-domain global modulation. Experiments on Moving MNIST, TaxiBJ, and WeatherBench demonstrate that WaveSFNet achieves strong prediction accuracy with low computational overhead.
